questions: Which exact component under `src/tools/video-editor/components/TimelineEditor/` owns a single clip/track row and should receive the `budget 2` instrumentation if `ClipRow` no longer exists?

For multi-instance surfaces such as `MediaGalleryItem`, should the overlay aggregate counts by component name, or is per-instance display expected even though `useRenderBudget(name, budget)` has no instance identifier?
Should `console.warn` fire only once per 1-second interaction window when a component first exceeds its budget, or on every subsequent over-budget render inside that same window?
Is `Ctrl+Shift+R` currently unused in the app, or should the overlay choose a different global shortcut after keybind inspection?

success_criteria.criterion: `src/shared/dev/useRenderBudget.ts` exports `useRenderBudget(name: string, budget: number): void`, performs all telemetry work only under `import.meta.env.DEV`, resets after 1 second of idle time, and emits the specified warning when a budget is exceeded inside the active window.

`src/shared/dev/RenderTelemetryOverlay.tsx` is mounted only in dev, is hidden by default, persists open/closed state in `localStorage`, responds to a non-conflicting global keybind, and displays live instrumented component counts with under/over budget status.

All required surfaces are instrumented, including the verified timeline row component, and the committed budgets are calibrated to observed baseline behavior rather than left at values that warn during normal interactions.

`docs/state_management_refactor_baselines.md` records the capture date, commit SHA, and measured baseline counts for at least five components across at least three applicable interactions, with narrative interaction-sequence notes for the key flows.
`npx tsc --noEmit`, `npm test`, and `npm run build` all succeed after the telemetry work is complete.
A grep of the built `dist/` assets finds no `RenderBudget` or `RenderTelemetryOverlay` strings.
The implementation stays within `src/shared/dev/`, `src/app/App.tsx`, `docs/`, and the explicitly instrumented component files, with no unrelated refactors or state-management changes.

The overlay uses existing Tailwind theme tokens instead of hardcoded colors, and the implementation avoids adding a new store/context or extra support files beyond the two approved dev files unless inspection proves they are required.
Manual preview-build smoke testing confirms persisted overlay visibility and at least one synthetic over-budget warning before the PR is opened.

assumptions: `src/app/App.tsx` is the correct shared mount point for the overlay unless repository inspection shows a higher root layout is required.

The lowest-risk design is a module-scoped dev registry inside `src/shared/dev/useRenderBudget.ts`, reused by the overlay, instead of introducing Zustand, React context, or any additional telemetry file.
Baseline numbers will be captured from a production preview build (`npm run build` plus the repo’s preview command), and HMR/dev-server counts will be ignored for documentation.
If no stronger product requirement appears during inspection, non-applicable table cells in the baseline doc can be recorded as `-`.
If a listed file path or component name does not exist as written, work should stop long enough to map the real equivalent before adding instrumentation.

If the brief does not answer the multi-instance question, the overlay should aggregate live instances by component name and show the highest active count for that name, because the hook API does not include an instance id.

Synthetic warning validation can be done by temporarily lowering one budget during manual verification, provided the committed code restores the calibrated final budget before the last build/test run.

The shell-inspection failure in this harness is environmental rather than repo-specific, so the plan relies on the paths and constraints supplied in the brief until normal local inspection is available.
